name: Go — Hybrid-Barrier Tricolor Concurrent Mark-Sweep
summary: Go's GC is concurrent non-generational, non-compacting tricolor mark-sweep with a hybrid Yuasa+Dijkstra-style write barrier; design is explicitly latency-first ('minimize latency, not maximize throughput').

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Go's GC is **concurrent non-generational, non-compacting tricolor mark-sweep** with a hybrid write barrier; the design is explicitly latency-first ("minimize latency, not maximize throughput"). The most consequential design choice is *not having generations* — Go bet that the generational hypothesis would not hold for its actual workloads, and the bet has held up.

Trade-off is no compaction (potential fragmentation), no generations (every GC is full-heap), but excellent tail latency and predictable behaviour.

sections.title: Hybrid Yuasa+Dijkstra barrier
sections.content:
The hybrid Yuasa+Dijkstra-style write barrier was introduced around **Go 1.8** to eliminate the stack-rescan STW phase. Yuasa's snapshot-at-the-beginning component shades the *overwritten* reference; Dijkstra's incremental-update component shades the *new* reference. Combining both lets the collector avoid both stack rescans and double-precision marking.

Later releases continued refining pacing, assists, and latency behavior. STW phases (mark/sweep termination) typically stay short.

sections.title: GOMEMLIMIT and pacing
sections.content:
**`GOMEMLIMIT`** (Go 1.19) — soft absolute heap cap with a GC-CPU governor preventing thrashing; complements the ratio-based `GOGC`. Pacer redesign work and assist-credit accounting; **allocate-black** + size-segregated 8 KB spans per-P eliminate locks in the common path.

The combination of `GOGC` (relative trigger) + `GOMEMLIMIT` (absolute cap) + GC-CPU governor (prevents pathological thrashing) is the operational latency story: services can opt into a memory ceiling without risking GC livelock when allocation pressure exceeds the heap cap.

sections.title: No-compaction, no-generations design lesson
sections.content:
Trade-off is no compaction (potential fragmentation), no generations (every GC is full-heap), but excellent tail latency and predictable behaviour. The most consequential design choice is *not having generations* — Go bet that the generational hypothesis would not hold for its actual workloads, and the bet has held up.

The lesson generalises: **the generational hypothesis is workload-specific**. Go workloads (network services, request handlers, heavy struct allocation) do not necessarily follow the "most allocations die young" pattern that justifies generational GC overhead in JVM workloads.
